title: Accusative Prepositions
description: Learn words like 'for', 'through', and 'without'—and how they force the Accusative case!
explanation:
**HOOK**
In English, prepositions are just little words like 'for' or 'with'. In German, these words are like invisible hands that reach out and pull the very next word into a specific Case! They are so bossy that the sentence doesn't even get to decide—the preposition does it for you.

**CONCEPT**
We are learning 'Akkusativ-Prepositions'. These words are special because they ALWAYS trigger the Akkusativ case, no matter what the verb is doing.

**RULE**
Just remember the 5 Bosses: durch (through), für (for), gegen (against), ohne (without), um (around). If you see these, use 'den', 'einen', or 'mich/dich' immediately after!

tables.title: The Bosses (5 Master Prepositions)
tables.note: TIP: All of these force the word after them into Akkusativ.

deep_notes.title: WATCH OUT
deep_notes.text: Remember: In Akkusativ, only the MASCULINE changes. So if it's 'für die Frau' or 'für das Kind', it stays exactly the same as Nominativ! But for men, it's 'für den Mann'.

exercises.question: Du spielst Tennis und dein Freund ist dein Rivale. Du spielst ____ den Freund.
exercises.hint: You are rivals. You play 'against' him.

exercises.text: Der Mann (er) hat Geburtstag. Das ist für ____.
exercises.hint: It belongs to the man (er). 'für' forces Akkusativ.

exercises.question: Jemand ist dir sehr wichtig und du gehst nirgendwo hin, wenn er nicht dabei ist:
Ein Geschenk ist oft ____ eine spezielle Person gemacht.
